statement: Gerhard Schröder, Chancellor of Germany from 1998 to 2005, became the most prominent example of post-government 'revolving door' controversy in European politics. Within weeks of losing the 2005 federal election, Schröder accepted the position of chairman of the shareholders' committee of Nord Stream AG, the consortium building the Nord Stream 1 gas pipeline from Russia to Germany — a project he had championed as Chancellor. He subsequently joined the board of TNK-BP (2009), took a role with Nord Stream 2 AG (2017), and was nominated for the board of Russian state oil company Rosneft (2017), a position he held until stepping down under pressure in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Schröder's close relationship with Russian President Vladimir Putin drew sustained criticism across the German political spectrum. The European Parliament passed a resolution in 2022 calling for sanctions against Schröder. His case has become a standard reference in debates about cooling-off periods and post-government ethics rules, including in Iceland's EU accession debate.
source_name: European Parliament Resolution 2022/2658(RSP); German Federal Parliament (Bundestag) records; major international news sources
caveats: Schröder's case is exceptional and not representative of typical post-government careers. Many former European leaders have gone into private sector roles without controversy (e.g., former EU Commission President Barroso's move to Goldman Sachs drew criticism but of a different nature). The relevance to Iceland's EU debate is limited: the question is whether EU membership would create specific revolving-door risks for Icelandic officials, which depends on the roles available and the cooling-off rules in place.

related_entries.statement: Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) introduced a bill in Althingi proposing an 18-month cooling-off period that would prevent senior officials and ministerial advisors involved in negotiations with international organisations from accepting positions with those organisations after leaving office. The bill was introduced in the context of potential EU accession negotiations, framed as a safeguard against conflicts of interest. Similar 'revolving door' regulations exist at the EU institutional level: EU Commissioners face an 18-month cooling-off period (extended to 24 months for the Commission President), and the European Ombudsman has called for stricter enforcement. Several EU member states, including France and Germany, have analogous rules for senior civil servants.

Iceland has an established legal framework for managing conflicts of interest in government. Key instruments include: (1) Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum í Stjórnarráðinu (Act on Prevention of Conflicts of Interest in Government Offices), which requires disclosure of financial interests and imposes restrictions on officials' participation in decisions where they have personal interests; (2) Siðareglur ráðherra (Code of Ethics for Ministers), which establishes standards of conduct for cabinet members; (3) General administrative law principles on disqualification (vanhæfi) under the Administrative Procedures Act (stjórnsýslulög nr. 37/1993). However, Iceland does not have a post-employment cooling-off period requirement for ministers or senior officials — there is no mandatory waiting period before former officials can take positions in the private sector or with international organisations. This gap has been highlighted in international assessments, including GRECO (Group of States against Corruption) evaluation reports.

Many Western democracies have stricter post-employment (revolving door) restrictions than Iceland for senior government officials. Examples include: France requires a 3-year cooling-off period for senior officials before joining companies they supervised, enforced by the Haute Autorité pour la Transparence de la Vie Publique (HATVP); Canada imposes a 2-year post-employment restriction for ministers and 1 year for senior officials, enforced by the Office of the Conflict of Interest and Ethics Commissioner; the European Commission imposes a 2-year cooling-off period for departing Commissioners (extended to 3 years for the Commission President), during which they must notify and receive approval for any new professional activities; the United Kingdom has an Advisory Committee on Business Appointments (ACOBA) that reviews post-employment moves by ministers and senior officials, typically recommending 6–24 month waiting periods (though compliance is voluntary). By contrast, Iceland has no mandatory cooling-off period for ministers or senior officials, a gap highlighted in GRECO evaluations.
